event: 55 years old
male
history of nonalcoholic steatohepatitis
history of alpha 1 antitrypsin deficiency
underwent orthotopic liver transplant
postoperative course was complicated by an incarcerated inguinal hernia
incarcerated inguinal hernia was repaired uneventfully
postoperative course was complicated by cytomegalovirus (CMV) viremia
received treatment with valganciclovir
discharged on maintenance immunosuppression of mycophenolate mofetil, tacrolimus, and prednisone
presented 5 weeks post-transplant
2 days of encephalopathy
increasing home oxygen requirements
required 4L nasal cannula to maintain oxygen saturation >92%
encephalopathy characterized by sparse speech and disorientation with a nonfocal neurologic examination
white blood cell count was 9.3 × 109/L
creatinine 2.12 mg/dL
blood urea nitrogen of 53 mg/dL
synthetic liver function international normalized ratio, alanine aminotransferase, aspartate aminotransferase, and bilirubin were within normal limits
arterial ammonia was unusually elevated at 204 µmol/L
induction dosing of intravenous ganciclovir was started
started on empiric antibiotic coverage with vancomycin, meropenem, micafungin
intravenous micronutrient supplementation for B1, B6, and levocarnitine was initiated
lumbar puncture on admission revealed an opening pressure of 8 cmH2O
Gram stain revealed encapsulated yeast suspicious for Cryptococcus
started on liposomal amphotericin B and flucytosine
started on continuous renal replacement therapy (CRRT) and rifaximin, zinc, and lactulose
ammonia proceeded to unexpectedly climb to 692 µmol/L
empiric intravenous doxycycline was started
Cryptococcal serum and cerebrospinal fluid (CSF) antigen titers both returned positive at >1:2560
cultures from bronchoalveolar lavage, CSF, and blood all revealed cryptococcal growth
repeat lumbar punctures revealed opening pressures greater than 45 cmH2O
required large volume drainage every 48 hours
persistent thrombocytopenia that developed 48 hours into his hospitalization
reaching a nadir of 16 × 103/µL
mental status transiently improved
unable to wean from mechanical ventilation
required tracheostomy
continued to display complications of cryptococcal infection
related to sludging and obstruction of microvascular structures
resulting in persistent hydrocephalus
oliguric renal failure
and progressive splenic infarcts with necrosis requiring splenectomy
Magnetic resonance imaging of the brain did not reveal cytotoxic edema
his hospital course was further complicated by sepsis
duodenal leak
persisting renal failure
and failure to thrive

revealing a low urine orotic level
